item.title: how i make sprites out of nodes

description.p: i find myself often sharing how i make sprites directly in godot rather than an art program, so here's how i do that :p

godot has infected my mind in irreversible ways. my notes are taken in trees, most of my ui demos are in godot, and my hair is starting to get weird bumps . okay but all jokes aside i do actually have a reason (whether or not it's good? ill, uh, let you decide)
- avoids rasterization

godot doesn't actually render SVGs as vectors. it'll first rasterize it (turning it into pixels) and then draw that. the new-ish DPITexture helps but it's imperfect, and according to godot themselves not a full solution.
- super easy animation

maybe a hot take! i like AnimationPlayer! i use it for almost everything. the way my artstyle works, i could compose stuff out of svgs but i'd have to do sprite stacking for all sprites, and if i wanna animate some new element, i have to go back and split it up. with my method, i can animate literally anything in a sprite and it'll look totally radical
- i dont like svg editors

maybe a hot take2! i DON'T like SVG software. i haven't found one that i don't fit slightly annoying and in-my-way-all-the-damn-time. maybe adobe illustrator is good. i'm not finding out. the best i've found is krita but i honestly say that for everything surrounding it's vector capabilities, not specifically the vector stuff. this feeds into the upper point, if i have to go back and split up an svg, i have to use svg software and i think inkscape now flinches everytime i look at it
- automation

last but honestly maybe most, i know godot very well and i am very good at automating my tools in ways i couldn't with an SVG editor. there's very little difference between my greyboxing and final levels in explosive potato because frankly, nothings is hand placed. it'd be harder to have it in 2 stages than to just... make the level without the flowers or grass (which i've also thought of automating). of course this also goes into individual sprites like the player, i've automated alot of the art stuff (that i can get away with)
if you aren't used to handling *lots* of nodes... uh you'll be in for a treat. for reference here's the barrel's scene tree

if performance sounds like a concern, it probably won't be. i easily get over 60fps on my thinkpad t580 with only integrated intel uhd 620 graphics, and even if you use this technique my project probably uses heavier graphics effects then you'll need
the other issue, if you're making outlines, can be outlines... i've been making mine manually, which depending on complexity can be really challenging
description.h2: basics of how to do it
description.p: I use a mixture of built in nodes and my own custom nodes. I'll go over the built in ones first:
- ColorRect
useful for solid rectangles of color
- Panel

useful for slightly more complex rectangles and parallelograms, i.e. if they have rounded corners or outlines. I'd also check out the StyleBoxFancy extension which let you do alot of cool stuff as you'll see
- Polygon2D
useful for, you guessed it, polygons
- Line2D
yeah okay we get it i think
There's also some useful properties to look out for, i.e. CanvasItem.clip_children which lets you make a parent act as a mask for it's children.
The most important one in this group is my RingDraw class. this lets you draw circles and rings, and i probably use it more than anything
in case you've never seen @tool or _draw(), here's some guides:
- running code in the editor (@tool)
- custom drawing in 2d (_draw())

Another is Spline. It's a way of turning a bezier curve into a Line2D, and this is done via Path2D (mostly because it conveniently uses bezier curves and has an editor for them, so we don't have to do that)
another cool part to this node type, is that it lets you interpolate the spline, so for example you could make, for example, a wind trail
when working with polygons, "offsetting" is making them grow/shrink in size. this class just adds a simple way to quickly do that (helpful with outlines, mostly)
- use Node2D to make folders / bones

you can almost think of this as a rig at certain points. using Node2D as a parent can make it really easy to add pivots to different parts of the body of the sprite. if you go back to the scene tree i had shown earlier, i do this for the different segments of the baarrel as i wanted the lid to shake when it was close to exploding. the scene tree is your friend!
- this doesn't mean use no images
i use images all the time for icons or more complex shapes i cant easily make even with my advantages. you don't lost the advantages mentioned by using them
- PLEASE. use good naming conventions

navigating these trees gets alot easier if you put in that extra 5% to make the tree navigable, believe it or not. if you've been around the block you're probably already good at naming conventions, but don't neglect it here just because this isn't programming
- @tool is your friend

as i mentioned, automation is one of the biggest advantages here. there's tons of scripts i've made that i haven't shared. (maybe i'll make another post on some of those eventually, my wall and brick generators are super interesting but too complicated for this post)
i've completely redone the website from the ground up, as i originally made my website when i first learned HTML and i think i can do alot better now.
the palette i'm using is one i made myself called vint-hs. it's heavily inspired by blank vhs boxes and their fun designs, though i dont think the website really reflects that.
the font used is fira code, a monospace font with ligatures that i really like. and the icons are from google's Material Symbols set.

all the source code is on github, if you wanna see it. that's all i have for now :] i do plan to use the blog more, and i actually have a workflow that makes sense for it :p i think it'll mostly be godot tutorials and updates on my current online PvP project, explosive potato! join the discord server if you wanna see more consistent stuff about that.

item.title: first blog post :]
item.description: (click to see full) first blog post :], admittedly im not sure if i want this to be a super common thing where i talk about random stuff or just updates to the site or project announcements but we'll see... there's a chance i forget i made this after a week but im hoping not :p
